blocks.content: I am writing on a train.

As much as I want to say it’s a good vibe and all, with the constant small jerky movements, I would be surprised if this journal entry actually ends up being straight, or if I can even finish it without getting nauseous. I do not understand how people used to do this.

To be honest, I’m debating about what made me happiest today. On one hand, there is something really heartfelt that made me really happy. On the other hand, something really funny happened too, which equally made me happy.
Even as I just wrote those last two sentences though, I already know which one I’m going to write about:
the funny one.
Why? Because everything I’ve been writing about recently has been quite deep and/or heartfelt. I think writing about the funny one would be nice for a change.

To give some context, my brother, our cousin, our uncle, and I went boating today. I had taken some very cool shots of the camera coming out of the water (and had at the time somewhat decided to write about that as the happiness thing). It was all just very peaceful and nice.
But as we were floating along, we all noticed that something else was also floating along, not so far from where we were. As we neared this floating object, it slowly dawned on us what it was:
a dead fish.

My younger cousin immediately jumped up, full of excitement, and shouted, “I want to catch it!” He went to the front of our boat, rope in hand, and began swinging it as if he was going to catch the fish using the rope. Obviously, that did not work, so he opted to do something that I did not think he would actually do until he did it:
he picked up the fish with his bare hands.
Wow, I’m using a lot of colons today.

Now, keep in mind, this fish was at least 10-15 pounds and had likely been dead for quite a while. Yet, he’s still tried. But given the weight and slipperiness of the fish, it kept slipping out of his hands. At this point, my brother and I were dead from laughing. With my brother manning the boat, we drove around the fish multiple times, trying to get the fish onto the boat to no avail.

Why my cousin had suddenly decided to make it his life mission to catch this fish is something I am still unclear about. But after a few failed attempts, my uncle started trying too, eventually flopping the fish onto the middle of our small boat.
The gratification was instant, but so was the smell.
After a couple of seconds, we had all unanimously decided to throw it off the boat.
The smell still haunts me.

But thinking back to those moments, I realize how purely happy we all were. We had, at least for a few minutes, forgotten about everything else, solely focusing on catching the fish. The moment that smelly fish landed onto the boat, we had all rejoiced, and even though the moment had only lasted for a few seconds (and the smell for a few hours), it felt so worth it for the joy we felt.
Now here comes the deep stuff because I can’t help myself.
I think in certain moments in life, do the dumb stuff that makes you happy. Even if it means the smell now makes you gag a little at anything that remotely taste/smells like fish.
Dance at the party.
Raise your hand to give that funny answer or ask a funny question.
Speak fluent English to those random Chinese strangers.
Laugh at the person snoring on the train (for a respectful amount of time, of course).
Because more often than not, those are the things that become core memories. The story you tell for years to come. The thing that you end up writing in your journal.

While they might be fleeting, these memories give you a holistic, positive view of things. That day might’ve had its ups and downs, but it will be that funny moment you end up remembering the most by the end of the day. Those better days will then be the ones that determine those great weeks; those weeks, months; those months, years.
Because like those cliché posters say, laughing is just as important as living and loving.
